findings.title: Locale-dependent decimal separator silently corrupts TF-IDF index and WAL scores
findings.evidence: awk 'printf "%.4f", val' is locale-aware. On macOS with LC_NUMERIC=ru_RU.UTF-8 (user's live locale per `locale`), output is '4,7004' not '4.7004'. The index file contains 'body\tregex-chars:4,7004' after running `bash hooks/memory-index.sh` in the sandbox. Downstream consumers do `split($j, fp, ":"); fp[2]+0` (session-start.sh L394, score-records.sh L98) — awk coerces '4,7004' to integer 4, losing precision. Same issue in hooks/lib/score-records.sh L74 for WAL spaced-repetition scores ('name:4,7:3' parsed as 4).
findings.reproducer: LC_NUMERIC=ru_RU.UTF-8 awk 'BEGIN{printf "%.4f\n", 4.7}'  # prints 4,7000 not 4.7000. Then: echo 'name:4,7004' | awk -F: '{print $2+0}' → 4
findings.proposed_fix: Set LC_ALL=C at the top of every awk-heavy hook (memory-index.sh, score-records.sh, memory-analytics.sh, wal-compact.sh), or wrap printf calls with explicit locale reset.
findings.failing_test_hint: export LC_ALL=ru_RU.UTF-8; create two memory files; run hooks/memory-index.sh; grep any float in .tfidf-index — assert dot separator, assert awk '+0' on it yields the fractional value.

findings.title: Tab after YAML key (e.g. 'status:\tactive') preserves tab in value and breaks tab-delimited output
findings.evidence: sub(/^status: */, '') only matches 'status:' followed by space(s). A tab after the colon leaves the value as '\tactive'. Since the parser prints tab-separated fields, the embedded tab in status bleeds into adjacent columns. Test output for memory/mistakes/tabs-in-yaml.md: 'memory/mistakes/tabs-in-yaml.md\t\tactive\tglobal\t2\t0000-00-00\t\tmajor\t "Tab indented value"\t_empty_\t_none_\ttab\tdomain\tBody.' — 14 fields instead of 13; downstream `IFS=$'\t' read -r kscore file status ...` assigns file="", status="memory/mistakes/...md", recurrence="active", which corrupts every subsequent decision.
findings.reproducer: printf 'status:\tactive\n' | awk '/^status:/{sub(/^status: */, ""); print "[" $0 "]"}'  → [\tactive]. Create file with 'status:<TAB>active' in frontmatter, run AWK_MISTAKES, observe fields 2+ shifted.
findings.proposed_fix: Change all sub patterns in parse-memory.sh, user-prompt-submit.sh extract_meta, and session-stop.sh lifecycle_rotate from 'status: *' to 'status:[[:space:]]*'. Also gsub(/\t/, ' ', $0) before storing value.
findings.failing_test_hint: Create mistake file with tab after 'status:'. Assert AWK_MISTAKES output has exactly 12 tab-delimited fields and status field is 'active'.

findings.title: Unclosed frontmatter parsed as valid; file injected with empty body header
findings.evidence:
The AWK parser at L30 counts `---` occurrences. With only one `---` at top, fm_end_count never reaches 2, past_fm stays 0, but status/severity/recurrence etc. are all captured from the pseudo-frontmatter. Downstream (session-start.sh L261), status='active' and recurrence>=1 pass all filters. A malformed file 'unclosed-fm.md' produced injection output: '### unclosed-fm [major, x3]


' (empty body). User sees a blank mistake heading with severity/recurrence metadata but no content — misleading signal.
findings.reproducer: Create file with only top '---' and no closing. Run session-start.sh with this file in mistakes/. Check output additionalContext — the slug appears as a mistake heading with empty body.
findings.proposed_fix: Require fm_end_count==2 before treating status as active. In AWK_MISTAKES END block, skip records where past_fm==0 (no closing ---). Alternatively emit a SCHEMA_ERROR marker like user-prompt-submit.sh does.
findings.failing_test_hint: Create a mistake file missing closing '---'. Run session-start; grep MISTAKES_MD for the slug — assert it's absent.

findings.title: Quoted YAML values preserve quotes literally, propagating to injected context and breaking status checks
findings.evidence: sub(/^root-cause: */, '') leaves the value quoted. For `root-cause: "Test unclosed cause"` the captured value is literally `"Test unclosed cause"`. User sees `Root cause: "Test unclosed cause"` in injected context. Worse: for `status: "active"` the AWK captures `"active"`; then session-start.sh L261 `[ "$status" != "active" ] && continue` treats it as inactive and SKIPS the file. Same file is also skipped by lifecycle_rotate in session-stop.sh L75-107 (no match on any branch), so it never decays.
findings.reproducer: Create mistake with `status: "active"` quoted. Run session-start — file is never injected. Run session-stop — file is never marked stale/archived.
findings.proposed_fix: Add `gsub(/^["']|["']$/, "", <val>)` after every `sub(/^<key>: */, "")` in parse-memory.sh (mirroring what user-prompt-submit.sh extract_meta already does partially).
findings.failing_test_hint: Create mistake with quoted status/project. Assert session-start injects it. Then advance mtime 200 days; run session-stop; assert status flipped to stale.

findings.title: extract_meta lacks CRLF stripping — all Windows-edited files report SCHEMA_ERROR
findings.evidence: hooks/lib/parse-memory.sh AWK programs do `gsub(/\r/, "")` at L19/55; user-prompt-submit.sh extract_meta does not. A file with CRLF line endings has '---\r' as first line, which matches `/^---$/` in BSD awk only sometimes, and the closing '---\r' likewise. Tested output on memory/feedback/crlf-feedback.md: extract_meta returned 'SCHEMA_ERROR'. Result: WAL gains a 'schema-error' entry, file is never triggered, user assumes trigger doesn't fire.
findings.reproducer:
printf -- '---
type: feedback
status: active
project: global
trigger: "x"
---

Body
' > /tmp/crlf.md; then run the extract_meta awk from user-prompt-submit.sh against it — outputs 'SCHEMA_ERROR'.
findings.proposed_fix: Add `{ gsub(/\r/, "") }` as the first AWK rule in extract_meta, matching parse-memory.sh.
findings.failing_test_hint: Create CRLF-encoded feedback file with trigger. Send prompt matching the trigger; assert the memory gets injected via UserPromptSubmit hook.

findings.title: YAML block scalar (key: |) captures literal '|' and drops continuation lines
findings.evidence: For `root-cause: |\n  This spans\n  multiple lines`, the sub() leaves value='|'. Then the indented continuation lines don't match any `^<key>:` rule, so they're passed to the body-collection rule `past_fm && !/^$/` — but past_fm is still 0 (we're inside frontmatter), so they're dropped. The injected `Root cause: |` is misleading.
findings.reproducer: Create memory file with root-cause block scalar. Run AWK_MISTAKES — cut -f7 returns '|'. python3 also extracts '|' via bin/memory-dedup.py regex, causing false-merge risk (see R11).
findings.proposed_fix: Detect block scalar '|' or '>' suffix; either reject (SCHEMA_ERROR) or implement multi-line accumulation. Minimum: emit placeholder '_multiline_unsupported_' and warn in WAL.
findings.failing_test_hint: Create mistake with root-cause: |\n  line1\n  line2. Assert parser outputs either the joined text or a clear schema error — not literal '|'.

findings.title: Block-style YAML arrays (keywords:\n  - foo) lose all values
findings.evidence:
The AWK matcher `/^keywords:/` only handles inline `[a, b]` syntax via `sub(/^keywords: *\[?/, "")`. For block form:
```
keywords:
  - api
  - auth
```
`sub` returns empty string for `keywords:`, and subsequent `  - api` lines match no rule (in_fm is true but no `^<key>:` match). Tested output: keywords field is empty. Downstream keyword scoring gives 0 hits — file is effectively invisible to the ranker. This form is shown in the README templates indirectly (evidence: uses it).
findings.reproducer: Create feedback file with block-style keywords. Run AWK_SCORED — cut -f6 returns empty. Run session-start — no keyword boost for that file.
findings.proposed_fix: Extend AWK: when /^keywords:[[:space:]]*$/ toggle an `in_kw` flag; collect subsequent `/^  - (\S+)/` lines into keywords until another top-level key. Same for domains/triggers/evidence.
findings.failing_test_hint: Create feedback with block-style keywords matching a current session keyword. Assert score > 0 in awk_out of collect_scored.

findings.title: UTF-8 BOM at file start prevents frontmatter parsing
findings.evidence: File starts with EF BB BF 2D 2D 2D 0A — the opening '---' is preceded by 3 BOM bytes. The AWK regex `/^---$/` requires line content to be exactly '---', but BOM makes the first line's content '\ufeff---'. fm_end_count never increments to 1 for the opening, so frontmatter is never entered. Tested: bom-file.md produced all '_empty_' fields. In session-start.sh this file is silently dropped.
findings.reproducer:
printf '\xef\xbb\xbf---
type: mistake
status: active
---

Body
' > /tmp/bom.md; bash -c 'source hooks/lib/parse-memory.sh; awk "$AWK_MISTAKES" /tmp/bom.md' — shows '_empty_' status.
findings.proposed_fix: Add at very start of AWK programs: `FNR == 1 { sub(/^\xef\xbb\xbf/, "") }` or strip BOM from the whole document before the main rules.
findings.failing_test_hint: Write BOM-prefixed mistake file. Assert it is parsed and injected, indistinguishable from the BOM-less equivalent.

findings.title: Filenames with spaces corrupt INJECTED_SLUGS dedup check (case-glob false matches)
findings.evidence: build-context.sh builds `INJECTED_SLUGS=' file with spaces ...'` (slug includes spaces). The `case "$INJECTED_SLUGS" in *\" ${_target} \"*)` then falsely matches any single-word target that appears between the spaces, e.g. target='with' matches ' file with spaces ' — marks 'with' as already injected. Tested: all of 'file', 'with', 'spaces' report BUG: matched non-existent slug.
findings.reproducer: bash -c 'INJECTED_SLUGS=" file with spaces "; for t in file with spaces; do case "$INJECTED_SLUGS" in *" $t "*) echo MATCH-$t;; esac; done'
findings.proposed_fix: Either reject filenames containing whitespace at ingest time, or replace the space-separated slug list with an associative array (declare -A injected_set; injected_set[$slug]=1). Also sanitize slugs to `[A-Za-z0-9._-]+` before using them as identifiers.
findings.failing_test_hint: Create two mistakes: 'file with spaces.md' and 'file.md'. After session-start, the 'file' slug should still be processed as cluster/related — assert it appears in CLUSTER_MD when reachable.

findings.title: Large memory files (>2MB) blow past 15s SessionStart timeout
findings.evidence: Benchmarked in sandbox: a 3MB mistake file (50000 body lines) takes 13s through AWK_MISTAKES — essentially the entire hook timeout budget consumed by parsing one file. No cap on input size. Settings.json configures timeout=15s; a 3-5MB file would be truncated by Claude Code, partial output corrupts JSON. Real-world trigger: user dumps a large log/test-output into a mistake file.
findings.reproducer: seq 1 50000 | awk '{print "line"$1}' >> /tmp/big.md (after frontmatter). time awk "$AWK_MISTAKES" /tmp/big.md ≈ 13s on M-series macOS.
findings.proposed_fix: Clip input at file scan: check _stat_size before feeding to awk; if >512KB, truncate or skip with a schema-warning WAL entry. Alternatively, cap body concatenation in AWK: `if (length(body) > 16384) next`.
findings.failing_test_hint: Create a 5MB mistake, inject session-start input JSON. Assert hook completes in <5s and the file either is truncated in output or skipped with a wal-warning event.

findings.title: dedup.py extracts '|' for block-scalar root-cause → 100% similarity triggers false auto-merges
findings.evidence: re.search(r'^root-cause:\s*["\']?(.+?)["\']?\s*$', ...) against `root-cause: |\n  line1\n  line2` captures literal '|' (tested in sandbox: Match: '|'). token_set_ratio('|', '|') == 100. Any two new mistakes with block-scalar root-causes pass the MERGE_THRESHOLD=80 check and auto-delete the newer one via `new_file.unlink()`. Data loss.
findings.reproducer: Python: token_set_ratio('|', '|') → 100.0. Then create two mistakes both with root-cause: |\n  ...; run dedup.py on the second — it's deleted.
findings.proposed_fix: After regex match, reject or warn if extracted value equals '|' or '>'. Better: use a YAML parser (ruamel.yaml) for authoritative extraction. Minimum guardrail: require `len(root_cause.strip()) >= 10`.
findings.failing_test_hint: Two mistakes with distinct multi-line block-scalar root-causes. Run dedup on the newer; assert it is NOT deleted.

findings.title: dedup.py crashes with FileNotFoundError on broken symlinks in mistakes/
findings.evidence: mistakes_dir.glob('*.md') returns symlinks; f.read_text() raises FileNotFoundError if the symlink target is missing. Tested output: full traceback on stderr, exit 1. hooks/memory-dedup.sh then treats that as non-2 exit, does `[ -n "$OUTPUT" ] && echo "$OUTPUT"` — pushes the Python traceback into Claude's context as a dedup warning.
findings.reproducer: ln -s nonexistent.md memory/mistakes/broken.md; run bin/memory-dedup.py memory/mistakes/<some-new>.md — FileNotFoundError traceback.
findings.proposed_fix: In the glob loop: `if not f.is_file(): continue` OR `try: ... except (FileNotFoundError, OSError): continue`.
findings.failing_test_hint: Create a broken symlink in memory/mistakes/. Invoke memory-dedup.py on an unrelated new file. Assert exit 0 and empty stdout (no traceback).

findings.title: Horizontal rules (---) in body corrupt body capture (no whitespace between paragraphs)
findings.evidence: When body contains a stand-alone `---` line (markdown HR), the rule `/^---$/ { fm_end_count++ }` fires but never hits `if (fm_end_count == 1 or 2)`, so the line is NOT consumed via `next`. Falls through to `past_fm && !/^$/ { ... body = body $0 \x1e }`, appending the literal '---' to body. The output body for the test file: 'Body has a horizontal rule next:---After separator.' — three paragraphs collapsed.
findings.reproducer: Create mistake file with '---' on its own line in the body. Parse with AWK_MISTAKES — body contains the literal '---' without the surrounding newlines.
findings.proposed_fix: Add `fm_end_count >= 2 && /^---$/ { body = body $0 \x1e; next }` to protect post-frontmatter `---` lines from being mistreated, OR treat subsequent `---` as skip-line when past_fm.
findings.failing_test_hint: Mistake with body containing '---' as HR. Assert the body includes the HR on its own line when reconstructed.

findings.title: find_memory_file ignores nested subdirectories in mistakes/ — cluster links fail
findings.evidence: find_memory_file does `for dir in ...; do f="$MEMORY_DIR/$dir/${slug}.md"; [ -f "$f" ]`. This is flat — no recursive search. But session-start collects with `find $MEMORY_DIR/mistakes -name '*.md' -type f` which recurses. A file at mistakes/subdir/nested.md is injected, but build-context.sh forward-scan calls find_memory_file('nested') → returns rc=1, so cluster expansion and contradicts detection silently fail.
findings.reproducer: Create mistakes/subdir/nested.md with `related: - foo: instance_of`. Run session-start: nested.md injects, but its related 'foo' is never expanded because find_memory_file can't locate 'nested' reciprocally.
findings.proposed_fix: Either forbid subdirs at ingest (install.sh or a linter), or change find_memory_file to use `find "$MEMORY_DIR/$dir" -name "${slug}.md" -type f | head -1`.
findings.failing_test_hint: Create mistakes/deep/a.md and mistakes/b.md with reciprocal `related:`. Assert cluster expansion shows both.

findings.title: Symlinked memory files silently excluded by find -type f
findings.evidence: find flags `-type f` does NOT match symlinks to regular files. If a user symlinks mistakes/shared.md → /some/elsewhere/shared.md, `find memory/mistakes -type f` omits it. The system behaves as if the memory does not exist — no warning, no WAL event. Tested: valid symlink excluded from results; `find -L` would include it.
findings.reproducer: ln -s /path/to/real.md memory/mistakes/linked.md. Run session-start: linked.md is never considered.
findings.proposed_fix: Decide policy: either (a) `find -L ... -type f` to follow symlinks, or (b) additionally match `\( -type f -o -type l \)` and filter out broken links with `[ -e "$f" ]`. Document the choice in hooks/lib/parse-memory.sh header.
findings.failing_test_hint: Symlink a valid memory file into mistakes/. Assert it's processed.

findings.title: Empty array `keywords: []` becomes empty string (not the `_none_` sentinel)
findings.evidence: `sub(/^keywords: *\[?/, "")` turns `keywords: []` into `]` → then `sub(/\].*/, "")` turns it into ``. The default is `_none_`, but `keywords: []` overrides to empty. Downstream domain_matches is OK (empty → match all), but keyword-scoring AWK at session-start.sh L242 uses `if (fkw != "_none_" && n > 0)` — an empty fkw would attempt split, producing fks[1]=\"\". No loop iterations, so no score — but it's distinct from _none_ semantics. The TF-IDF injection path (L420) checks `if (fkw == "_none_")` before awarding TF-IDF bonus; for empty string, the bonus is skipped. So files with `keywords: []` get neither keyword score nor TF-IDF bonus — worst of both.
findings.reproducer: Create feedback with `keywords: []`. Check collect_scored — the file has score 0 regardless of TF-IDF index content.
findings.proposed_fix: In AWK_SCORED/AWK_MISTAKES, after the sub/gsub for keywords, set `if (keywords == "") keywords = "_none_"`. Same for domains.
findings.failing_test_hint: File with empty keywords array, high TF-IDF score on body. Assert the file still scores > 0.

findings.title: memory-dedup.sh passes unsanitized session_id to python — WAL line can be broken by '|' or newline in id
findings.evidence: Line 32: `export HYPOMNEMA_SESSION_ID="${SESSION_ID:-unknown}"`. No `|`→`_` or `/`→`_` substitution (unlike memory-outcome.sh L27 and session-start.sh L586). python bin/memory-dedup.py L70/77 writes `w.write(f"{today}|dedup-merged|{new_name}>{existing_name}|{session_id}\n")`. If session_id contains `|`, the line has extra fields; if it contains newline, a second garbage WAL line appears.
findings.reproducer: Pipe `{"session_id":"abc|def\nghi","tool_input":{"file_path":"/tmp/mem/mistakes/new.md","content":"..."}}` into memory-dedup.sh. Observe WAL lines.
findings.proposed_fix: Sanitize before export: `SAFE_SID="${SESSION_ID//|/_}"; SAFE_SID="${SAFE_SID//$'\n'/_}"; export HYPOMNEMA_SESSION_ID="$SAFE_SID"`. OR sanitize inside the python script.
findings.failing_test_hint: Craft input with session_id containing '|'. Assert only one WAL line written, and it has exactly 4 fields.

notes: Sandbox location: /tmp/hypomnema-audit-sandbox (cleaned up). All findings reproduced against the live parse-memory.sh, build-context.sh, score-records.sh, evidence-extract.sh, user-prompt-submit.sh extract_meta, memory-index.sh, memory-dedup.sh, bin/memory-dedup.py. Confirmed graceful-degradation for: zero-byte files (parsed as all-empty, skipped by status filter), corrupt projects.json (detect_project returns empty via jq empty check), read-only files during perl -pi (errors swallowed by 2>/dev/null), missing MEMORY_DIR (session-start exits 0 early; session-stop finds no files). The most impactful class is the BSD awk `sub(/^key: */)` pattern family — it survives mostly-well-formed ASCII YAML but falls over on tabs, quotes, block scalars, block arrays, and BOM. R1 (locale) has the widest blast radius because it silently corrupts every numeric persisted score for non-en_US users — user himself runs Russian locale (see env output at start of audit).
